title: SQL for Data Wrangling
text:
SQL stands for Structured Query Language, and is a way to define, manage, and manipulate databases. In this tutorial, we will walk through basic SQL syntax and patterns that cover many data analysis tasks: filtering rows with WHERE, summarizing with GROUP BY, and combining tables with JOIN. Knowing these three patterns opens the door to answering many questions directly in the database.



Before we write full queries, it helps to understand the basic pieces that show up in almost every SQL statement. At a minimum, you usually need two parts:
SELECT columns_you_want
FROM table_name;

SELECT tells the database which columns to return
FROM tells it which table to read from




The WHERE clause lets you filter a table down to only the rows you care about. You can still use SELECT to choose columns and FROM to choose the table, but WHERE adds a logical condition on top.
Example:
SELECT name, gpa
FROM students
WHERE major = 'STAT' AND gpa >= 3.5;
In this query: - SELECT name, gpa chooses the columns to return - FROM students says we are reading from the students table - WHERE major = 'STAT' and gpa >= 3.5 keeps only rows where the major column is exactly STAT and the GPA is at least 3.5
You can combine conditions with logical operators like AND, OR, and NOT:
SELECT *
FROM students
WHERE major = 'STAT' OR major = 'CS';



So far, our queries have worked at the row level. Often we want summaries by groups: “average GPA by major” or “total sales per customer”. The GROUP BY clause lets us aggregate many rows into one row per group.
The basic pattern is:
SELECT group_column,
    AGG_FUNCTION(other_column) AS summary_name
FROM table_name
GROUP BY group_column;
Suppose we had a table with the following columns: - id_number - name - major - gpa
To compute the average GPA by major:
SELECT major,
    AVG(gpa) AS avg_gpa
FROM students
GROUP BY major;
This query produces one row per major. Within each major, the AVG function is applied to all rows in that group and returns the result as avg_gpa. Other possible functions to be used here include COUNT, SUM, MIN, MAX.



Databases rarely keep everything in one big table. Instead, related information is split across tables and linked by key columns. The JOIN clause lets you combine those tables so you can analyze them together.
The most common pattern is an inner join:
SELECT *
FROM table1 AS t1
JOIN table2 AS t2
    ON t1.key = t2.key;
Imagine we have two tables.
Table 1 (students) variables: - student_id - name - major
Table 2 (enrollments) variables: - student_id - course - grade
To see each student’s name alongside their grade for a given course, we can join on the student ID:
SELECT s.name,
       e.course,
       e.grade
FROM students AS s
JOIN enrollments AS e
    ON s.id = e.student_id
WHERE e.course = 'STAT386';
HERE JOIN ... ON s.id = e.student_id tells the database how the two tables are related: match each row in students to enrollments with the same id. After the join, the WHERE clause filters down to just the course we care about. This pattern can be chained so there are multiple tables joined in a single query.



In this tutorial, you saw how a few SQL patterns can cover a lot of everyday analysis work. You learned the basic shape of a query with SELECT, FROM, and WHERE, how to filter rows and columns using conditions, how to summarize by different groups using GROUP BY and aggregate functions like AVG, and how to combine related tables using JOIN. Together, these patterns let you pull exactly the data you need from a database instead of exporting huge CSV files and cleaning everything later.
Next, try making these ideas concrete in your own environment, Start by writing a simple query that filters a table with a WHERE clause, then turn it into a grouped summary with GROUP BY. After that, practice joining two tables into a grouped summary with GROUP BY. After that, practice joining two tables that share a key to answer a question you couldn’t answer from either table alone.

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text: I am an avid runner and I recently introduced my husband to Strava. We went to the gym one day and he stopped to take a selfie to add to his Strava post. He said that he gets more Kudos (the equivalent to a ‘like’) when he adds a photo. After I made fun of him for that statement, I was wondering how true that is? As two people with few followers (48 for me and less for him on his new account), is there really a difference? What about a strava post invites more kudos from my friends? As someone who unfortunately seeks validation from others, I wanted to find out what exactly does make a more kudos-worthy post?
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text: I am an avid runner and I recently introduced my husband to Strava. We went to the gym one day and he stopped to take a selfie to add to his Strava post. He said that he gets more Kudos (the equivalent to a ‘like’) when he adds a photo. After I made fun of him for that statement, I was wondering how true that is? As two people with few followers (48 for me and less for him on his new account), is there really a difference? What about a strava post invites more kudos from my friends? As someone who unfortunately seeks validation from others, I wanted to find out what exactly does make a more kudos-worthy post?
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
Motivating Question

Do Strava activites with photos receive more kudos than activities without photos?
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
Ethics
Strava has a very user friendly API with clear documentation on their website. In order to ethically use this, I had to create an application. Strava used to have an open api where anyone could call methods without authentication. This created an uncontrollable and untrackable load on their servers. This caused them to “close” their API to give them more control over who accesses the API and how they do it. To abide by their new guidelines to avoid this, I registered an ‘application’ which essentially is my permission to access the API. Additionally to avoid overloading their systems, I made sure to only run the code every 15 minutes since it was scraping such a large set of data.
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
Steps

1. Register a Strava Application
To access the API, I registered an application at:
Strava API Settings
This process generates several credentials needed to authenticate requests, including:

Client ID

Client Secret

Access Token

The access token allows my program to make requests on behalf of my Strava account.
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
2. Authenticate with the Strava API
Using the authentication documentation provided by Strava, I generated an access token that allowed my script to retrieve activity data. For data privacy, I stored this in a personal credentials file that I called in.
The authentication guide can be found here:
Strava Authentication Documentation
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
3. Retrieve Activity Data
Using Python and the requests library, I called the Strava endpoint:
/athlete/activities
This endpoint returns information about the activities associated with the authenticated athlete.
Since Strava only returns a limited number of activities per request, I implemented pagination to retrieve all available activities.
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
4. Extract Relevant Variables
From each activity returned by the API, I extracted several features relevant to my question:

Activity ID

Activity name

Distance

Moving time

Total elevation gain

Kudos count

Photo count

Whether the activity contained a photo

Activity date

These variables allow me to compare engagement (kudos) across activities with and without photos.
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
Overview of the Final Dataset
After collecting the data, I constructed a dataset where each row represents a single Strava activity from my personal account.
Dataset size

Observations (activities): 783

Features: 8


Data Dictionary



Variable
Description




activity_id
Unique identifier for the activity


name
Name given to the activity


date
Date the activity occurred


distance
Distance of the activity (miles)


moving_time
Total moving time (minutes)


kudos_count
Number of kudos received


photo_count
Number of photos attached to the activity


has_photo
Indicator variable (1 = photo present, 0 = no photo)
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
Data Cleaning and Processing
The raw API data required only minimal cleaning. The main transformations included:

Creating the has_photo indicator variable for later analysis

Converting distance from meters to miles

Converting date to YYYY-MM-DD format

Exporting the final dataset to a CSV file for analysis
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
Limitations of the Data
The main limitation for this dataset is that I can only call the data from my personal account which provides insights that may not generalize to other users.
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?
text:
Code Repository
The full code used to collect this dataset can be found here: Strava Authentication Documentation Github Strava Repository
crumbs: Does Posting a Photo on Strava Get You More Kudos?

title: About Me
text:
I am an Applied Mathematics student with a minor in Statistics, passionate about turning messy data into clear insights. With a strong foundation in mathematics, Python, R, and SQL, I am actively seeking opportunities to grow as a data scientist. I love using technical skills to support data-driven decision-making.
I have experience in data science through both internships and research, and I am comfortable learning new tools and data systems quickly. I am especially interested in what data can reveal and predict, and I’m excited to bring curiosity, initiative, and a commitment to high-quality work to the workforce.
